title: Letters to the Editor
summary: The Justice Department defended the roadless rule in federal court in Wyoming but did not appeal U.S. District Judge Clarence Brimmer's decision to strike it down in July. The environmental groups had intervened in the case and did appeal. They are asking the 10th U.S. Circuit Court of Appeals to reinstate the rule, which blocked road construction in 58.5 million forest acres nationwide as a way to stop logging and other commercial activity. President Clinton created the rule in the final days of his administration. It affects 3.5 million acres in Wyoming. In his ruling, Brimmer said Clinton's rule was a "thinly veiled attempt" to create wilderness areas without congressional action. In a friend-of-the-court brief, the Justice Department argued that since it was an executive branch rule Br…

Hughes thought, 'I'll make something to hang the cord on and paint it blue, and then it'll be a 'cord on blue.' " Hughes displays two prototypes, at right, and the final product, left, of the Cord on Blue, a device that can hold cords and hoses. (Photo by Fran Durner / Anchorage Daily News) J.P. title: Letters to the Editor
summary: Gold sold in 1980 for as high as $850 an ounce but fell to well below $300 by 2001. The industry saw a few bull markets, with prices reaching $400, $500 and $600 an ounce, but prices began to fall again in 1996, said Greg Johnson, vice president of corporate development for NovaGold Resources Inc. The Vancouver, British Columbia-based company is involved in potential Alaska gold mines such as the Rock Creek project near Nome and the Donlin Creek project in southwestern Alaska. Finding a way to encourage smaller-scale businesses such as placer mining outfits was a focus at the annual Alaska Miners Association convention held in Anchorage earlier this month. title: Letters to the Editor
summary: The report traces the flows of federal money and identifies patterns and change in federal spending from 1983 through 2002. It shows significant increases in spending corresponding with Stevens' taking over as chairman of the Appropriations Committee, which is responsible for annually allocating more than a half-trillion dollars in federal funds. Before Stevens became chairman in 1997, per-capita federal spending levels in Alaska had ranged between 20 percent and 50 percent over the national average. By 1999, per-capita spending levels in Alaska had increased to more than 70 percent above the national average, the report said. Stevens has doggedly used his position to benefit Alaska and frequently is criticized by other lawmakers and good-government groups as a pork-barrel spender. The ins…

title: Know your commercial real estate paperwork
summary: The sale and purchase of commercial real estate involves numerous, and sometimes confusing, documents. Here are some typical documents you can expect. Some of this information comes from an article, "Do You Need a Commercial Loan?" by Stan Mullin and Don Smith in the fall 2002 trade magazine Professional Report. • Letter of Intent : The letter is a simplified offer that describes the basic economic terms of the offer and is signed by both buyer and seller. It typically includes purchase price, cash at closing or seller financing, buyer's due diligence inspection items and time frame, and earnest money amount. It is replaced by a purchase and sale agreement. • Purchase and Sale Agreement : This is the full agreement for the purchase and sale of the property and is signed by both the buyer a…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: Detectives from Barrow to Juneau already share information, but it can take hours or days to run down a single lead over the phone, sometimes only to find out it's a dead end. Coplink will allow investigators to sift through thousands of law enforcement records around the state with just a few keystrokes, saving valuable time, the software's owner says. Coplink is designed on the notion that most crimes are committed by people who already appear in police records, said Bob Griffin, chief executive officer of Knowledge Computing Corp., which produces the software. With Coplink, detectives are able to search that data to come up with leads they might not otherwise have known about, or at least not as quickly, he said. Detectives can enter small shreds of information gathered from witnesses -…

summary: Prices to fishermen from the 2003 season totaled $5.7 million, up from $4.6 million in 2002, because of a failed run. "This is the only area-wide commercial fisheries program that is benefiting the area. We are producing the highest quality salmon in the state, other than troll caught." Under the co-op program, 77 of Chignik Bay's 100 seiners stopped competing against one another and agreed to fish cooperatively. The other 23 fish independently. title: Letters to the Editor
summary: So far, veterans in the Barrow region have agreed to start a chapter or post and other groups in Minto, Nenana and Fort Yukon have committed to similar efforts, Cleveland said. Kotzebue, Kake and the Kenai area also have shown interest. The Alaska Native Veterans Association started in the 1980s but wasn't federally recognized and granted nonprofit status until 2000, Cleveland said. "We've been kind of struggling along," he said. With the latest effort, Cleveland hopes to change that. Not only will the association have links to the villages, it will be able to gauge the numbers of veterans in Alaska, Native or non-Native, giving the organization a larger voice in the state and nationally. He said in the past it has been hard for veterans to travel to Fairbanks or Anchorage for treatment or…

The amount of snow that broke away from a slope above him on Triangle Peak, where Hoyt was climbing with two friends, could fit in the bed of a pickup, as he described it. title: Letters to the Editor
summary: More than two-thirds of 150 Haines respondents to the random survey said they favored improved ferry service over a road to Juneau. When a West Lynn Canal road option was added, 53 percent chose improved ferry service, with 33 percent in favor of the road. Only 9 percent of Haines respondents favored the Murkowski administration's stated favorite option, an east-side road. Haines Borough Mayor Mike Case said he was not surprised by the results, but he doubts that the state will give Haines' preferences much weight. "People generally use surveys to support decisions they've already made," Case told the Chilkat Valley News. "If they can't use them for that, then they usually say the results aren't accurate. It all goes into the mix." Skagway reported numbers similar to Haines, with 53 percen…

summary: Ritchie summarized components of the league's stance on state fiscal issues. The policy statement was unanimously approved by delegates Friday afternoon, he told the Fairbanks Daily News-Miner. The meeting, an annual conference of local governments, ran Wednesday through Friday. About 140 of the state's 161 municipalities belong to the league. Ritchie said the league believes a state income tax may be needed to solve the state's fiscal problems and the state should not impose a sales tax. That tax should be left to the municipalities. The league is supporting the percent of market value system of management for the Permanent Fund. "We spent a lot of time talking about it," Ritchie said. Under the system, the state would be able to access 5 percent of the total value of the Permanent Fund e…

AT&T technician Doug Kyle said because of weather conditions, the helicopter that dropped Cooley and Earls off couldn't stay. title: Letters to the Editor
summary: Before he was named head of emergency services, the elder Krill served as fire chief. The younger Krill is a full-time Anchorage firefighter who also works as a part-time firefighter in Mat-Su, where he lives. A perception that nepotism played a role in the younger Krill's appointment has elicited some grumbling among the borough's rank-and-file firefighters. "He was groomed for this position," said Kurt Jarmer, a volunteer firefighter who recently left the department. "His dad is director of public safety." But borough officials insist that the younger Krill got the job over deputy Mat-Su fire chief James Steele fair and square. The younger Krill is a battalion chief in the Mat-Su department. title: Letters to the Editor
summary: In the 1960s, more than 80 percent of U.S. workers had health insurance through their employers. Today, in the face of skyrocketing premiums, that's down to 62 percent. Some big firms, like Wal-Mart, offer only catastrophic plans to cover primarily life-threatening situations. Others are requiring workers to pay significantly higher portions of their care, both in terms of premiums and deductibles. The combination has fueled a sharp increase in the number of uninsured who work in large firms. Thirty-two percent of the uninsured, or nearly 1 in 3, now work for companies that employ 500 workers or more. That's up from 25 percent in 1987, according to a recent report from the Commonwealth Fund. Experts say that sends warning signals about the state of the traditional private health-insurance …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: Why? Let us count the reasons:. Number One -- Wrong Target : Any effort to boycott Alaska tourism over this issue will only hurt supporters of bears and wolves. The only people who will abide the boycott are the people who support the tourism businesses run by wolf and bear lovers. Bubba Joe Salmon Slayer from Minneapolis isn't going to stay home no matter how many bears and wolves Alaskans want to kill. title: Letters to the Editor
summary: As technology allows faster access to information, it has also allowed insurance companies to more efficiently evaluate their risk and maximize their premiums through data-mining technology -- the use of the Comprehensive Loss Underwriting Exchange, or CLUE, database and "insurance credit scores." About 90 percent of insurance companies send a variety of claim and damage information into a central processing point every month. A CLUE insurance company member can then search five years of information cross-referenced with an applicant's name, address (proposed, current and previous), date of birth and Social Security number. Concerns have arisen regarding the accuracy of the information and the effect it can have an individual's and property's insurability. When evaluating insurability, sev…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: Pungowiyi, one of the few nonscientists at a recent international gathering of Arctic researchers in Seattle, described how the extraordinarily warm weather in the Arctic over the past decade is disrupting fishing, animal behavior, the ice-based system of winter travel and their entire way of life. "Our whole system is based on the cycle of nature," he said. "I keep thinking it will soon get back to normal, but it doesn't." Why the Arctic is not normal is the primary focus of the Seattle meeting. Organized by the National Science Foundation as part of its project SEARCH (the Study of Environmental Arctic Change), it is the largest international gathering of researchers studying a variety of disturbing changes in the Arctic such as thinning sea ice, shifts in ocean currents and disruptions …

summary: In Alaska News More headlines » National | World | Business | Sports State tries to balance its budget and needs By LISA DEMER Anchorage Daily News (Published: November 6, 2003) Lawmakers absorbed an earful Wednesday about problems and successes within the state agency responsible for serving Alaska's most vulnerable and needy residents. State Sen. Fred Dyson, R-Eagle River, called the joint public hearing before the Senate and House committees on health, education and social services to hear about peoples' experiences with the Department of Health and Social Services. Dozens of people participated by phone links from Kotzebue to Juneau. Most were those who depend on therapies, counseling, assistance in the home, or health care paid for through the department, or who worked for an agency t…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: In the pre-dawn dark, Burkmire said, it was easy to see the shower of sparks bouncing off the road as Woods flicked his ash from his cigarette once, then about a half-mile up the road flipped the glowing orange "cherry" of burning embers off the cigarette's tip. Woods swerved a little, his 1992 Dodge pickup briefly crossing the white fog line and the rumble strip, and then swerved back to the center, Burkmire said. The trooper pulled him over at Mile 38 of the Glenn, near the gravel pits on the way into Palmer. Woods was fine and perfectly sober, Burkmire said, but none too happy to be cited for littering. "He basically denied throwing his cigarette out the window because he still had the butt in his hand," he said. "He denied that was actually littering." Woods, reached at his home Thursd…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: Vacant Kmart building sold Developer sees architectural opportunities By WESLEY LOY Anchorage Daily News (Published: November 7, 2003) The vast roof of the former Kmart near Northway Mall stretches into the distance around commercial real estate developer John Blomfield on Thursday afternoon in East Anchorage. Blomfield has purchased the building, which, according to his calculations, encompasses an area equivalent to 3.2 football fields. (Photo by Erik Hill / Anchorage Daily News) Click on photo to enlarge An Anchorage commercial real estate developer has bought the vacant Kmart building on the east side of town and hopes to lease it to a new retailer or convert it for office space. It's a whole bunch of building to work with, 170,000 square feet on 12 acres, and John Blomfield said he ca…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: Such a punitive tax would be viewed by the industry as adversarial, especially at a time when Alaska wants to attract more oil and gas investment to the state, he said. The tax would be on the North Slope's natural gas reserves -- the known gas still in the ground -- and would be aimed at getting production going so the tax could be avoided. The governor sees a reserves tax as the wrong way to get the multibillion-dollar project built, said his spokesman, John Manly. People need to understand the long and costly legal battle that would ensue, Manly said, adding that the companies' response likely would be, "We'll see you in court and for many years to come." But Alaskans are growing tired after waiting 25 years for a gas pipeline, Croft said. If a project makes economic sense, and if the t…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: "We've got $26 million to find," board member Tim Steele said. "If we don't find it in small stuff, we're going to have to find it in personnel and I'm very reluctant to do that. These are not (decisions) that are going to be popular. I know that. But I think responsible management dictates we look at those things." District officials and board members will continue pruning the budget through the end of the year. The school administration usually brings a proposed budget to the School Board for public hearings and action in January. The $26.2 million gap is about 7 percent of the anticipated $397.3 million in general fund expenditures. Expected revenue of about $371.1 million assumes the district is allowed to collect $133.9 million in local taxes, the maximum allowed under Anchorage's tax…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: Amanda Sauro, 2003 class president of Barlett High School, received a $3,000 scholarship from the National Rifle Association (NRA) and National Foundation for Women Legislatures (NFWL). In August, Sauro was sent to Las Vegas, where she gave a speech regarding the essay she wrote. Its theme was "Women of Valor." Sauro wrote about the herioism of Joan of Arc and how women of all generations can gain courage from her legacy. Her essay was among the top six nationwide out of more than 500 essays. This money will go toward her studies in political science at the University of Alaska Southeast in Juneau this fall semester. Peter Iwuoha of Chester Valley Elementary School won a ride to and from school on a fire truck earlier this year. He and his family turned in the winning entry in the Great Es…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: DESCRIPTION: A highway community where nearly all of the 33 homes are occupied year-round. The Little Nelchina State Recreation site at Mile 137.6 offers camping and a boat launch. The Nelchina Trail store and cabins offer convenience items and snowmachine support. HISTORY: Gold in the creeks draining from the Chugach Mountains brought prospectors to the area in the late 1800s. The area offered several trails into the mountains. Nelchina (an Athabascan Indian name) was a mining settlement established around 1913. It was first reported by Theodore Chapin in a 1915 U.S. Geological Survey publication. The Anchorage Daily News - Get the whole story every day - Sign up for home delivery! title: Letters to the Editor
summary: Native Heritage Center gives elders their day TRADITION: Project under way to record the stories of the older generation. By ZAZ HOLLANDER Anchorage Daily News (Published: November 2, 2003) Frances Johnson, 93, center, celebrated Elders Day at the Alaska Native Heritage Center with her great-granddaughter Deana Schubert, 12, and her daughter Carolyn Schubert. Johnson, from the Golovin Bay area, wears the most recent of the seven traditional dress parkas she has sewn. (Photo by Marc Lester / Anchorage Daily News) Kevin Evan dances the Tug-O-War Dance with the Alaska Native Heritage Center dancers during Elders Day there Saturday. title: Letters to the Editor
summary: To be eligible for a block heater installation, timer and cord for $25, car owners must live in the Anchorage Bowl between the Glenn Highway's Muldoon on-ramp and the Potter Marsh weigh station on Seward Highway. Vehicle owners who already have a block heater are eligible for a free programmable timer. For an appointment to install a block heater, call Jerry's Auto Service, 7327 Spruce St., at 344-0238 or 344-2735; or Agen's Automotive, 737 E. International Airport Road, at 563-2436. To receive a free timer, drivers may bring their car vehicle registration to the Environmental Services customer service desk, 8:30 a.m.-4:30 p.m. Monday through Friday, at the Municipal Department of Health and Human Services, 825 L St. For more information, visit www.careaboutair.com , call the hot line at 3…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: Jury indicts Udd on '02 Hummer accident ASSAULT: Aces owner denies he had a seizure. By PETER PORCO Anchorage Daily News (Published: November 4, 2003) This 2003 Hummer, owned by Rodney Udd, who owns the Hummer of Alaska dealership in Anchorage, was one of six vehicles involved in a wreck on DeBarr Road near Alaska Regional Hospital on Aug. 26, 2002. (Photo by Bill Roth / Anchorage Daily News archive 2002) Click on photo to enlarge Rodney Udd, owner of Anchorage Chrysler Dodge Center and an owner of the Alaska Aces hockey team, has been indicted on assault charges stemming from a vehicle crash last year. summary: Alyeska engineers anticipated the effects of a bruising quake WELL-DESIGNED: Pipeline took temblor with little damage. Scientists hope that seismic lessons learned from 7.9 earthquake a year ago, in a state with one person per square mile can be applied to places where strike-slip faults cut through heavily populated areas.
